sentence: Subjective:
The patient is a 56-year-old male presenting with difficulty swallowing, which he describes as a sensation of something being stuck in his throat, occurring over the last four to five weeks.
He reports that this sensation is more pronounced with solids and can be painful.
He has had no coughing, vomiting, or blood in his stool.
He denies any weight loss or changes in his weight.
His past medical history includes diabetes and high blood pressure.
Review of systems: The patient denies any symptoms in other organs that are relevant to his initial presentation.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of diabetes and high blood pressure.
He is currently taking metformin 500mg twice a day for diabetes and norvasc for high blood pressure.
Current medications: Metformin 500mg twice a day for diabetes, Norvasc for high blood pressure.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 120/50 mmHg.
Physical exam: The patient's neck is fine, with no swelling, and his thyroid feels normal.
There is no lymphadenopathy.
The throat examination is normal, with no masses or swelling.
The lung exam is clear, and the heart exam is normal.
There is epigastric tenderness on the right side, but no masses or significant swelling.
The back exam is normal, and the extremity exam is fine.
The neurological exam is normal.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient is a 56-year-old male with difficulty swallowing due to a possible narrowing of the esophagus, which may require dilation.
The patient also has diabetes and high blood pressure.
Problem list: Difficulty swallowing; diabetes; high blood pressure.
Plan:
I recommended that the patient undergo a gastroenterology consultation for further evaluation and possible dilation of the esophagus.
I also prescribed Prilosec 20mg once a day for possible gastroesophageal reflux disease.
I advised the patient to chew their foods frequently and carefully, especially with hard or sticky foods.
I ordered a repeat hemoglobin A1c, kidney function tests, and a referral to ophthalmology for diabetic retinopathy screening.
I also refilled the patient's norvasc prescription.
Follow-up:
I will see the patient back in about a month to review the results of the blood tests and make any necessary adjustments to his treatment plan.
